guest: Sheel Mohnot, co-founder of a top-level-domain auction company and Better Tomorrow Ventures; also founded and sold The Pitch podcast to Gimlet

business_ideas.idea: An ascending-clock second-price auction platform to resolve contention for new top-level domains (.app, .blog, etc.) among competing bidders
Replica Apple iPod-mini colored earbuds sourced cheaply from China and sold online to students
Buying cheap multi-unit rental properties (Pittsburgh, Chicago, SF) for cashflow
Options trading on public internet/travel companies using Google Trends data as an earnings-surprise signal
Buying Uber ad-words traffic cheaper than the referral payout to arbitrage promo codes
A new venture fund, Better Tomorrow Ventures, focused on fintech

predictions.text: .app will become one of the biggest and most valuable new top-level domains.

dynamics: Single host (Shaan Puri) drives with rapid, curious follow-up questions and injects his own parallel anecdote (a live bankruptcy-auction bidding war for the Bebo domain) to compare notes with the guest's expertise. Energy is high, fast-paced and playful, with mutual riffing on hacks and schemes; guest supplies most of the technical/business detail while host translates it for the audience.
frameworks: Ascending clock second-price (Vickrey-style) auction: bidders drop out at their max price; winner pays just above the second-highest bid, incentivizing honest bidding
The Six/Seven Ts investing framework: Team, Technology (need), Total addressable market, Traction, Terms, totally random (like the people), and Faith
learnings: New top-level domains (.app, .blog, etc.) created a scarce asset class where multiple large companies (Google, Amazon, billionaires) had to bid against each other, creating demand for a neutral auction mechanism.
A well-designed auction (ascending clock, second-price) removes the incentive to bluff and tends to produce the fairest, not necessarily the highest, price.

Persistence and willingness to physically show up (attending global domain conferences, buttonholing a reluctant party by literally taking up smoking to bond with him) can unlock deals that cold outreach can't.
Entry valuation matters enormously for angel-investing returns — investing at a $5M cap versus a $12M cap for otherwise similar deals roughly halves the ultimate multiple.
A founder's unrelated bootstrapped business in an adjacent niche (import data) can be strong evidence of execution ability before the 'real' company has any traction.
Time spent living in genuine poverty/hardship (a year on $1/day doing microfinance work in India) reshaped how the guest thinks about impact investing and appreciation for privilege.

banger_quotes.quote: I sold internet real estate to the tune of half a billion dollars worth.
For the first time in my life, this is what I'm meant to be doing.
This looks like this is probably what they were pre-approved for.

numbers_dropped: Sold half a billion dollars worth of top-level domains
Application fee was $185,000 per domain; ICANN received about 2,000 applications (~$360M in fees)
Top auctions went for over $100 million; cheapest went for around half a million
.photography domain sold for roughly $3 million and generated ~$5M in registration revenue in its first year
Bought his first Pittsburgh rental house for $47,000 with an $8,000 down payment
Sold 11,000 knockoff iPod-mini headphones (bought for under $1 each, sold for $15-20 each)
Sam/Shaan's Bebo bankruptcy bidding war escalated from $150,000 to a winning bid of $1,015,000
A $25,000 angel check into Flexport's seed round (at a $10M cap) is now worth over $2 million, an ~87x return
notable: The host interjects his own extended personal story (a live bankruptcy auction for the Bebo domain/assets) partway through, turning part of the episode into a shared story rather than a pure guest interview.
